hero.title: 🧠 NIS MICRO
hero.subtitle: The intuitive mind is a sacred gift and the rational mind is a faithful servant - Albert Einstein

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which statement best distinguishes innate immunity from adaptive immunity?
a: Innate immunity provides immediate non-specific protection
Innate immunity is mediated only by antibodies
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity provides rapid, non-specific protection against infection.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Immunity is best defined as:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Immunity refers to resistance against disease and infection.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: The immune system is composed of:
a: Cells, tissues, and molecules that mediate resistance to infection
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The immune system includes cells, tissues, and molecules that defend against infection.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which type of immunity provides immediate protection against microbial invasion?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity acts immediately upon exposure to pathogens.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Adaptive immunity differs from innate immunity because it:
a: Develops more slowly and is highly specific
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Adaptive immunity develops more slowly and targets specific antigens.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which cells mediate cell-mediated immunity?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Cell-mediated immunity is mediated by T lymphocytes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Humoral immunity is primarily mediated by:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Humoral immunity is mediated by antibody-producing B lymphocytes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which of the following is a major innate immune cell?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Neutrophils are key cells of the innate immune system.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which feature is characteristic of innate immunity?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity provides an immediate response to pathogens.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which molecule is part of innate immunity?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Lysozyme is an innate antimicrobial enzyme found in secretions.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Adaptive immunity recognizes antigens primarily through:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Adaptive immunity uses B-cell and T-cell receptors for antigen recognition.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Innate immune recognition of microbes is based on detection of:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Innate immunity recognizes pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs).

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) are primarily associated with:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: PRRs are receptors of the innate immune system that detect PAMPs.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: TLR-2 primarily recognizes:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-2 recognizes peptidoglycan from Gram-positive bacteria.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: TLR-4 is the principal receptor for:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-4 recognizes LPS from Gram-negative bacteria.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: TLR-5 recognizes which microbial structure?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-5 recognizes bacterial flagellin.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which Toll-like receptors recognize single-stranded viral RNA?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-7 and TLR-8 detect viral single-stranded RNA.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: TLR-3 is most important for recognition of:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: TLR-3 recognizes viral double-stranded RNA.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: NF-kB functions primarily as a:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: NF-kB is a transcription factor regulating immune-response genes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Activation of NF-kB results in:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: NF-kB promotes transcription of cytokines and immune-related genes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which of the following is considered a first-line physical barrier against infection?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Skin serves as a major physical barrier preventing microbial entry.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Lysozyme found in tears protects against infection by:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Lysozyme cleaves peptidoglycan in bacterial cell walls.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Respiratory cilia help prevent infection by:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Respiratory cilia clear microbes through mucociliary transport.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which innate immune cell is specialized for killing virus-infected cells?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Natural killer cells destroy virus-infected cells without prior sensitization.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which cell is the most abundant circulating phagocyte?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Neutrophils are the most abundant circulating phagocytic cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which cytokines are important in antiviral defense?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Type I interferons (IFN-alpha and IFN-beta) inhibit viral replication.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Transferrin, lactoferrin, and ferritin protect against microbes primarily by:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: These proteins limit microbial growth by sequestering iron.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: An inflammasome is primarily involved in:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Inflammasomes activate inflammatory mediators such as IL-1.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: The primary role of an antigen-presenting cell (APC) is to:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: APCs process antigens and present them to T lymphocytes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which of the following is a professional antigen-presenting cell?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Dendritic cells are the most effective professional APCs.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which cells are classified as professional antigen-presenting cells?
a: T cells, B cells, NK cells
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Professional APCs include dendritic cells, macrophages, and B cells.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which lymphoid organ is responsible for B-cell development?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: B lymphocytes mature within the bone marrow.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which lymphoid organ is responsible for T-cell development?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: T lymphocytes mature within the thymus.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which are considered primary lymphoid organs?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Primary lymphoid organs are sites of lymphocyte development.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Which are considered secondary lymphoid organs?
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Secondary lymphoid organs are sites of antigen-driven lymphocyte activation.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: Lymph nodes are specialized to initiate immune responses against:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: Lymph nodes initiate responses against antigens draining from tissues.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: The cortex of a lymph node is primarily the site of:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: B-cell follicles are located in the lymph node cortex.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: The paracortex of a lymph node is primarily the site of:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The paracortex is the T-cell-rich area of lymph nodes.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: The spleen primarily initiates immune responses against:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The spleen filters blood and responds to blood-borne antigens.

immunity_and_immune_system.q: The marginal zone of the spleen is located:
immunity_and_immune_system.l: The splenic marginal zone surrounds lymphoid follicles.

neuro_batch2.q: The Purkinje cells of the cerebellar cortex:
a: Provides the only output to the deep cerebellar nuclei
Receives inhibitory input from climbing fibers
Are excited by the axons of the basket cells
neuro_batch2.l: Purkinje cells are the sole output neurons of the cerebellar cortex and send inhibitory (GABAergic) signals to deep cerebellar nuclei.
neuro_batch2.h: Only output of cerebellar cortex → Purkinje cells

neuro_batch2.q: Lucy is a 55-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and diabetes for more than 15 years. While trying to get up from the floor, she noticed that she was unable to move her right arm or leg. Her speech was slurred and unintelligible. She lay on the floor and waited for help to arrive. At the emergency room, she was found to follow commands well, and although her speech was very slurred, it was logical in organization. The lower two-thirds of her face pointed to the right side when asked to protrude her tongue. Her right arm and leg were severely but equally weak. The left side was normal. Where is the lesion most likely located?
neuro_batch2.l: Equal contralateral face, arm, and leg weakness with dysarthria indicates a lacunar stroke of the internal capsule due to small vessel disease from hypertension and diabetes.
neuro_batch2.h: Equal face + arm + leg weakness → internal capsule (pure motor stroke)

neuro_batch2.q: Which of the following nuclei in the thalamus projects to area 3, 1, 2 of the cortex?
neuro_batch2.l: Area 3, 1, 2 is the primary somatosensory cortex. The Ventral Posterolateral (VPL) nucleus relays sensory information (pain, temperature, touch, proprioception) from the body to this cortical area.

neuro_batch2.q: Heschl’s convolutions are located in which of the following areas?
neuro_batch2.l: Heschl’s gyrus (transverse temporal gyri) represents the primary auditory cortex, located in the superior temporal lobe (Brodmann areas 41 & 42).

neuro_batch2.q: A 55-year-old overweight man collapsed while loading a truck. Examination revealed paresis of both right limbs with a Babinski sign on the right. The patient’s tongue deviated to the left upon protrusion, and he had no vibratory sense on the left side of the body. These findings suggest:
a: A lesion in the left medial medulla
A lesion in the right medial pons
A lesion in the right medial medulla
A lesion in the lateral medulla
A lesion in the left medial pons
neuro_batch2.l: Medial Medullary Syndrome (Dejerine syndrome) involves the pyramid (contralateral hemiparesis), medial lemniscus (contralateral vibration loss), and CN XII (ipsilateral tongue deviation). Tongue deviates toward the lesion side (Left).
neuro_batch2.h: Contralateral motor/sensory + Ipsilateral tongue = Medial Medulla

neuro_batch2.q: Symptoms of cerebellar disease include all of the following EXCEPT:
neuro_batch2.l: The cerebellum coordinates movement but does not initiate it. Lesions cause ataxia, intention tremors, and dysarthria, but NOT paralysis (which is a sign of UMN/LMN lesions).
neuro_batch2.h: Cerebellum = Coordination; Motor initiation = Cortex (Paralysis ≠ Cerebellum)

neuro_batch2.q: A 40-year-old male has difficulty producing a smile from the left side of his face. Muscles are flaccid and the eyelids are open. Where do the cell bodies of origin within the CNS lie?
neuro_batch2.l: This describes a Bell's palsy or Lower Motor Neuron facial nerve (CN VII) lesion. The facial nerve nucleus is located in the lower pons.
neuro_batch2.h: Facial nerve nucleus (CN VII) → Lower pons

neuro_batch2.q: The lateral geniculate body:
a: Transmits visual information to the cerebral cortex
Is a visual motor reflex center
Relays auditory information to the cerebral cortex
neuro_batch2.l: The Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN) of the thalamus is the primary relay station for visual information from the retina to the primary visual cortex (BA 17).

neuro_batch2.q: A 50-year-old woman has right-sided spastic paralysis, loss of vibratory sense on the right, and internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) where the left eye would not adduct. Her tongue deviated toward the right. Where is the site of lesion?
neuro_batch2.l: The presence of Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia (INO) signifies a lesion of the Medial Longitudinal Fasciculus (MLF), which is a classic localizing sign for the pons.

neuro_batch2.q: A 62-year-old man presents with slow shuffling gait, stooped posture, masked facies, micrographia, and a resting pill-rolling tremor. What is the diagnosis?
neuro_batch2.l: Parkinson’s disease is characterized by the 'TRAP' mnemonic: Tremor (resting), Rigidity, Akinesia/bradykinesia, and Postural instability, along with masked facies and micrographia.
neuro_batch2.h: Pill-rolling tremor + Shuffling gait + Masked facies = Parkinson’s

neuro_batch2.q: A 70-year-old woman presents with the complaint of difficulty walking. The history reveals that the woman suddenly had problems while she was playing with her granddaughter at the local park. The examination reveals a slender, otherwise healthy woman who has ataxia and is unable to do the finger-to-nose and the heel-to-shin tests on the right, and has slurred, garbled speech. Which of the following specifically describes the pattern of movement in this woman?
neuro_batch2.l: Dysmetria is a cerebellar sign characterized by inability to accurately judge distance, leading to overshooting or undershooting during movements like finger-to-nose testing.

neuro_batch2.q: A patient who suffers from chronic alcohol abuse, which affects neurons in the cerebellum, will most likely:
neuro_batch2.l: Chronic alcohol abuse commonly damages the anterior vermis of the cerebellum, leading to gait ataxia (truncal instability).

neuro_batch2.q: Which cortical area is responsible for taste recognition?
neuro_batch2.l: Brodmann area 43 corresponds to the gustatory cortex responsible for taste perception.

neuro_batch2.q: Huntington’s disease is characterized by neuronal degeneration in which of the following structures?
neuro_batch2.l: Huntington’s disease involves degeneration of GABAergic neurons in the striatum (especially the caudate nucleus), leading to chorea.

neuro_batch2.q: Which of the following is an excitatory neuron within the cerebellar cortex?
neuro_batch2.l: Granule cells are the only excitatory neurons in the cerebellar cortex, while all others (Purkinje, basket, stellate, Golgi) are inhibitory.

neuro_batch2.q: A 35-year-old woman is brought to her family physician by her husband. The results of the examination reveal deficits that suggest the woman has had a precipitous vascular event. A CT is ordered, revealing a small stroke in the genu of the internal capsule. Which of the following fiber populations is most affected in this woman's stroke?
neuro_batch2.l: The genu of the internal capsule contains corticonuclear (corticobulbar) fibers that control cranial nerve motor functions.

neuro_batch2.q: The neural tract that connects Broca’s area (Brodmann’s 44) with Wernicke’s area (Brodmann’s 22) and regulates fluent speech is the:
neuro_batch2.l: The arcuate fasciculus connects Broca’s and Wernicke’s areas and is essential for coordinated speech; damage causes conduction aphasia.

neuro_batch2.q: Which dopaminergic pathway projects mainly to the caudate nucleus?
neuro_batch2.l: The nigrostriatal pathway carries dopamine from the substantia nigra to the striatum (caudate and putamen) and is essential for movement control.

neuro_batch2.q: A 63-year-old patient is seen in a clinic with the following symptoms: a resting tremor in the hand, a slow shuffling gait, and difficulty arising from a chair when seated. These symptoms could be caused by a lesion in the:
neuro_batch2.l: Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra causes Parkinson’s disease, characterized by tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia.

neuro_batch2.q: Gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA) is considered the predominant neurotransmitter in all of the following except?
neuro_batch2.l: Corticostriate fibers use glutamate (excitatory), whereas basal ganglia intrinsic pathways (striatal and pallidal) primarily use GABA (inhibitory).

neuro_batch2.q: All of the following are the signs of lateral pontine syndrome EXCEPT?
a: Ipsilateral pain and temperature loss from the face
Contralateral pain and temperature loss from the body
Contralateral loss of discriminative touch and vibration in the body
neuro_batch2.l: Lateral pontine syndrome affects spinothalamic and trigeminal pathways but spares the medial lemniscus, so vibration and discriminative touch are preserved.

neuro_batch2.q: Lucy is a 55-year-old woman who has had high blood pressure and diabetes for more than 15 years. One day, as she was reaching for a switch to put on the light she collapsed. While trying to get up from the floor, she noticed that she was unable to move her right arm or leg. Lucy attempted to cry for help because she was unable to reach the telephone; however, her speech was slurred and rather unintelligible. She lay on the floor and waited for help to arrive. Lucy’s son began to worry about his usually prompt mother when she didn’t arrive for his birthday. After several attempts to telephone her apartment without getting an answer, he drove there to find her lying on the floor. She attempted to tell him what had happened, but her speech was too slurred to comprehend. Assuming that his mother had had a stroke, the son called an ambulance to take her to the nearest emergency room (ER). A neurology resident was called to see Lucy in the emergency room because the physicians there likewise thought that she had a stroke. The resident noted that Lucy followed commands very well, and although her speech was very slurred, it was logical and organized. Also, the lower two-thirds of her face drooped on the right. Her tongue pointed to the right side when she was asked to protrude it. Her right arm and leg were severely, but equally weak; her left side had normal strength. Where did Lucy’s stroke most likely happen?
neuro_batch2.l: A lesion in the left internal capsule causes contralateral pure motor deficits (face, arm, leg) with dysarthria due to corticospinal and corticobulbar fiber involvement.

neuro_batch2.q: A lesion of the genu of the internal capsule on the right side could result in all but which of the following?
a: Inability to smile with the left side of the face on command
Deviation of the tongue to the left on protrusion
All of the above could result
neuro_batch2.l: The genu of the internal capsule contains corticonuclear fibers, so lesions can cause contralateral facial weakness, tongue deviation, and cranial nerve-related deficits like hyperacusis.

neuro_batch2.q: Which of the following thalamic nuclei receives important projections from the internal segment of the globus pallidus and from the contralateral cerebellar nuclei?
neuro_batch2.l: The ventral lateral (VL) nucleus of the thalamus receives input from the globus pallidus and cerebellum and relays motor information to the motor cortex.

neuro_batch2.q: Lesions of the superior parietal lobule interfere with the patient’s ability to combine touch, pressure, and proprioceptive impulses, so he or she is unable to appreciate texture, size, and form. This loss of integration of sensory impulses is called:
neuro_batch2.l: Astereognosis is the inability to recognize objects by touch despite intact primary sensory modalities, due to parietal lobe damage.

neuro_batch2.q: A 55-year-old overweight man was brought to the emergency room unconscious after he had collapsed while loading a truck. After he regained consciousness, an exam revealed paresis of both right limbs with a Babinski sign on the right. The patient’s tongue deviated to the left upon protrusion, with fasciculations present, and he had no vibratory sense on the right side of the body. These findings suggest?
a: A lesion in the medial medulla
A lesion in the medial pons
A lesion in the lateral medulla
A lesion in the medial mid brain
neuro_batch2.l: Medial medullary syndrome (ASA infarct) causes contralateral paralysis and vibration loss with ipsilateral tongue deviation toward the lesion.

neuro_batch2.q: A 55-year-old man with a history of hypertension has been in excellent health until he notices that he has vertigo and unsteadiness lasting for a week. He then developed nausea, vomiting, dysphagia, hoarseness, ataxia, left facial pain, and right-sided sensory loss. There is no weakness. On examination, he is alert, with a normal mental status. There is left ptosis. He has loss of pain and temperature sensation on the right arm and leg and decreased joint position sensation in the left foot. He is unable to walk. The analgesia and thermal anesthesia on the left side of the face in this case most likely resulted from a lesion of which structure?
a: The principal (chief) sensory nucleus of V
neuro_batch2.l: Lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome damages the spinal nucleus of CN V, causing ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation from the face.

neuro_batch2.q: A 55-year-old overweight man was brought to the emergency room unconscious after he had collapsed while loading a truck. After he regained consciousness, an exam revealed paresis of both right limbs with a Babinski sign on the right. The patient’s tongue deviated to the left upon protrusion, and he had no vibratory sense on the right side of the body. These findings suggest:
a: A lesion in the left medial medulla
A lesion in the right medial pons
A lesion in the right medial medulla
A lesion in the lateral medulla
A lesion in the left medial pons
neuro_batch2.l: Medial medullary syndrome causes contralateral paralysis and vibration loss with ipsilateral tongue deviation toward the lesion.

neuro_batch2.q: Descending projections from the midbrain participate in pathways that are directly involved in the inhibition of pain in the spinal trigeminal nucleus in the pars caudalis, and in the posterior horn of the spinal cord. This region of the midbrain contains many cells with opiate receptors. Which of the following is the most likely location of these cells?
neuro_batch2.l: The periaqueductal gray (PAG) in the midbrain contains opioid receptors and plays a key role in descending pain inhibition.

neuro_batch2.q: The inferior colliculi are considered to be:
a: Directly involved in and a part of the visual pathway
Directly involved in and a part of the auditory pathway
Directly involved in and a part of the olfactory pathway
neuro_batch2.l: The inferior colliculi are midbrain structures that serve as major relay centers in the auditory pathway.

neuro_batch3.q: The Kluver-Bucy syndrome is characterized by docility, psychic blindness (compulsion to examine objects visually, tactually, and orally), bizarre sexual behavior and hyper sexuality. One would find this syndrome after lesions of which of the following structures:
neuro_batch3.l: Kluver-Bucy syndrome occurs due to bilateral lesions of the temporal lobes (amygdala), leading to hyperorality, hypersexuality, and visual agnosia.

neuro_batch3.q: A 3-month-old infant was admitted to the pediatric ward because of a large head. The infant stayed quietly in bed most of the day. The circumference of the head was 44 cm (normal value for this age is about 40 cm). The fontanelles protruded, and the cranial sutures were separated. The skull could be transilluminated with a flashlight. Magnetic resonance imaging showed enlarged lateral, third, and fourth ventricles. The closure of the vertebrae was defective in the lower back (spina bifida). Treatment was surgical implantation of a catheter into the lateral ventricle with drainage into the peritoneum. Is this a communicating or Noncommunicating type of hydrocephalus?
a: Communicating; because the CSF drainage is impaired within the subarachnoid space or at the level of the arachnoid villi
Noncommunicating; the fluid communication between the ventricular system and the subarachnoid space is impaired
neuro_batch3.l: Noncommunicating hydrocephalus results from obstruction within the ventricular system preventing CSF flow to the subarachnoid space.

neuro_batch3.q: A lesion of the Para hippocampal uncus leads to?
neuro_batch3.l: The uncus is part of the olfactory cortex; lesions cause olfactory hallucinations (uncinate fits).

neuro_batch3.q: A 45-year-old man is admitted to the emergency department with a worsening headache, nystagmus, and gait ataxia. An MRI of the brain demonstrates a large infarction involving cerebellum. During the neurologic examination, the doctor asks the patient to first touch his nose and then the examiner’s finger. Which of the following phenomena is most likely to be seen with this test?
neuro_batch3.l: Finger-to-nose testing reveals intention tremor in cerebellar lesions due to impaired coordination.

neuro_batch3.q: A 77-year-old right-handed man with a history of atrial fibrillation and diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency department by his family. His son describes that when his father awoke that morning, he was unable to use words or sentences that made any sense. On examination, the patient has spontaneous, fluent speech, with normal grammatical constructs and prosody. However, the majority of what he says is meaningless. He is unable to follow commands, except to close his eyes. When asked to identify objects, he uses inappropriate substitutions of words. He also fails to repeat words and is unable to read. A lesion of which of the following areas would most likely account for his language deficits?
neuro_batch3.l: Wernicke’s aphasia results from lesions in the superior temporal gyrus, causing fluent but meaningless speech with poor comprehension.

neuro_batch3.q: A 59-year-old male suffered a stroke and later presented with a spastic paralysis. Which of the following structures was affected by the stroke that accounts for the spasticity?
neuro_batch3.l: Damage to the internal capsule affects corticospinal tracts leading to upper motor neuron signs such as spasticity.

neuro_batch3.q: A patient who suffered a stroke presents with a paralysis of the right side of the lower face, right spastic paralysis of the limbs, deviation of the tongue to the right with no atrophy, and no loss of taste from any region of the tongue. Which of the following structures was most likely affected by the stroke?
a: Internal capsule of the right side
Internal capsule of the left side
Base of the medulla on the right side
Base of the medulla on the left side
neuro_batch3.l: Contralateral UMN facial, limb, and tongue weakness without atrophy indicates a lesion in the opposite internal capsule.

neuro_batch3.q: 60 year old woman suddenly remarked that she was seeing double and felt a weakness in her left arm and leg. Her husband noticed that her right eyelid was drooping. At the hospital, she was awake, oriented, and articulate. Her visual fields were normal but here right eye deviated to the right. On attempted lateral gaze to the left only the left eye responded; only the left eye constructed in response to light. Upon smiling, there was a minor weakness on the left. The gag, corneal, and jaw jerk reflexes were normal as were the sensory examinations of the face and body. Motor strength was normal in the extremities on the right but reduced on the left especially in the arm where there was a heightened biceps reflex and resistance to passive stretch. Where is the site of lesion?
neuro_batch3.l: Findings suggest midbrain lesion (Weber syndrome) with CN III involvement and contralateral weakness.

neuro_batch3.q: In a particular case, one woman lost her concept of left, with regard to her body and surroundings. For example when applying lipstick, she completely neglects the left side of her face. When eating she complains that her portions are too small, because she only eats from the right side of her plate. Where is her lesion?
neuro_batch3.l: Right parietal lesions cause contralateral hemispatial neglect.

neuro_batch3.q: The main efferent fiber system exiting the Amygdaloid nucleus is the
neuro_batch3.l: The stria terminalis is the major output pathway of the amygdala.

neuro_batch3.q: 52 yr old man visits the clinic complaining of difficulty in tasting the food. He also has trouble smiling and not closing the eyes tightly. Through which of the following tracts or nuclei affected cranial nerve traverse.
neuro_batch3.l: Taste sensation is carried to the nucleus tractus solitarius, which receives input from facial, glossopharyngeal, and vagus nerves.

neuro_batch3.q: During development the midbrain vesicle will become the midbrain (mesencephalon). The cavity of the midbrain is known as the:
neuro_batch3.l: The cavity of the midbrain is the cerebral aqueduct, connecting the third and fourth ventricles.

neuro_batch3.q: The foramen of megandie empties into which of the following cisterns
neuro_batch3.l: The median aperture (foramen of Magendie) drains CSF into the cisterna magna.

neuro_batch3.q: An elderly female patient complains that she cannot taste the food she eats. A careful neurological examination reveals no evidence of peripheral damage of the taste receptors. The evidence suggests, instead, that here is selective damage to certain regions of the brainstem. Which of the following sites would include damage that could result in the selective loss of taste?
a: Spinal nucleus of the trigeminal nerve
Reticular tegmental nucleus of the pons
neuro_batch3.l: The nucleus tractus solitarius is the central relay for taste in the brainstem.

neuro_batch3.q: The inability to perform a movement or sequence of movements despite intact sensation, automatic motor output, and understanding of the task is a likely consequence of damage to what cortical area?
neuro_batch3.l: Damage to the premotor cortex causes apraxia—impaired motor planning despite intact strength and comprehension.

neuro_batch3.q: Interruption of what central nervous system pathways explains the loss of vibratory and position sense in the right leg?
a: Fasciculus cuneatus of the dorsal column-medial lemniscal pathway
Fasciculus gracilis of the dorsal column-medial lemniscal pathway
neuro_batch3.l: Fasciculus gracilis carries vibration and proprioception from the lower body (legs).

neuro_batch3.q: Patients with basal ganglia lesions can have either hyperkinetic or hypokinetic movement disorders. Which of the following diseases cause hyperkinetic uncontrolled involuntary movements which produce a random pattern of jerks and twists?
neuro_batch3.l: Huntington disease causes chorea—random, jerky hyperkinetic movements.

neuro_batch3.q: 77-year-old man is finding that he has more difficulty moving about for the past year. He is annoyed by a tremor in his hands, but the tremor goes away when he performs routine tasks using his hands. His friends remark that he seems more sullen and doesn't smile at them, but only stares with a fixed expression on his face. He has not suffered any loss of mental ability. Which of the following diseases is he most likely to have?
neuro_batch3.l: Parkinson disease presents with resting tremor, masked facies, and bradykinesia.

neuro_batch3.q: Neurotransmitter produced at the postganglionic parasympathetic synapses is ?
neuro_batch3.l: Parasympathetic postganglionic neurons release acetylcholine.

neuro_batch3.q: Horner's Syndrome is characterized by?
neuro_batch3.l: Horner syndrome includes ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis, and flushing due to sympathetic loss.

neuro_batch3.q: Hyperkinetic syndromes such as ballism, Athetosis and chorea are related to a problem of thalamic disinhibition related to which of the following neurotransmitters.
neuro_batch3.l: Loss of GABA inhibition leads to thalamic disinhibition and hyperkinetic movements.

neuro_batch3.q: A patient displays right paralysis of vocal cords, absence of the gag reflex on the right, and difficulty swallowing. A lesion at which location would explain these findings:
neuro_batch3.l: Nucleus ambiguus controls CN IX and X functions including swallowing and gag reflex.

neuro_batch3.q: Which of the following statements about the cerebellar cortex is TRUE?
a: Mossy fibers and climbing fibers are inhibitory
Climbing fibers arise from cell bodies in the superior olivary nuclei
Purkinje cells inhibit cells of the deep cerebellar nuclei
neuro_batch3.l: Purkinje cells are inhibitory and regulate output of deep cerebellar nuclei.

neuro_batch3.q: MRI scan reveals that a newborn infant has a Noncommunicating hydrocephalus. Both the lateral ventricles and the third ventricle are enlarged, but the fourth ventricle is of normal size. What is the most likely explanation for the infant’s condition?
a: A blockage of the foramen of Magendie
An occlusion of the central canal of spinal cord
A stenosis of the cerebral aqueduct
A tumor in the subarachnoid space
neuro_batch3.l: Aqueductal stenosis blocks flow between the third and fourth ventricles causing upstream dilation.

neuro_batch3.q: A patient delays initiation of movement, displays an uneven trajectory in moving her hand from above her head to touch her nose, and is uneven in her attempts to demonstrate rapid alternation of pronating and supernating movements of the hand and forearm. Which of the following regions most likely contains the lesion?
a: Vermal region of the anterior cerebellar lobe
Hemispheres of the posterior cerebellar lobe
neuro_batch3.l: Flocculonodular lobe lesions affect balance and coordination, leading to movement irregularities.

neuro_batch3.q: A 55 yr old male is brought in to the doctor by his wife. She says that he has been irritable for the past three months and is acting strange. She also claims and he supports that in the past two weeks he has been fidgety with his hands and now he is demonstrating some strange facial tics. The historical exam yields little information as this patient was an orphan and has no parental or family history. The physical exam showed normal reflexes and sensation, normal strength and no ataxia. There appeared to be mild hypotonia, but this was subjective. The one constant finding was an apparent restlessness during the exam and constant movements of the face. He was given a simple test of memory and attention where he performed below average, and according to his wife way below what she knew he could do. During the test he was irritable and had one outburst over a seemingly trivial event. Select the true statement concerning this patient.
a: he is in the early stages of Parkinson’s disease and his restless hands are the early signs of a resting tremor
In the advanced stage of this disease he will have degeneration in the caudate nucleus
he probably has a loss of dopamine producing cells in the substantia nigra
his condition will improve with the administration of L-Dopa
neuro_batch3.l: The patient’s symptoms are consistent with a movement disorder involving basal ganglia dysfunction where multiple statements regarding pathology and treatment (including dopamine loss and L-Dopa response) are correct.
neuro_batch3.h: Think: Basal ganglia disorder → dopamine involvement → L-Dopa helps

neuro_batch3.q: A 65-year-old man has loss of pain and temperature sensation on the right side of the face and on the left side of the body. Examination shows partial paralysis of the soft palate, larynx, and pharynx, and ataxia, all on the right. The most likely cause of these findings is thrombosis to which of the following arteries?
neuro_batch3.l: This presentation is consistent with right lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome caused by occlusion of the right posterior inferior cerebellar artery, leading to ipsilateral facial pain loss, contralateral body pain loss, nucleus ambiguus dysfunction, and ipsilateral ataxia.

neuro_batch3.q: A vascular occlusion of a region of the brain damaged the overwhelming numbers of fibers that supply the basal ganglia. Which of the following regions constitute the receiving area of the overwhelming majority of fibers that supply the basal ganglia?
neuro_batch3.l: The neostriatum (caudate nucleus and putamen) is the main input (receiving) area of the basal ganglia, receiving the majority of cortical afferents.

neuro_batch3.q: A 43-year-old male who began to display marked involuntary movements at times of rest was seen by a neurologist, who concluded that he was suffering from Huntington’s disease. Which of the following neurotransmitters was lost or reduced in this individual?
a: Substance P in the substantia nigra
ACh and GABA in intrastriatal and cortical neurons
neuro_batch3.l: Huntington’s disease involves degeneration of GABAergic neurons in the striatum, leading to reduced GABA and acetylcholine levels and resulting in hyperkinetic movements.

neuro_batch3.q:
A 65 year old black female was referred to a neurologist for evaluation of a movement disorder. Over the last 3 years the patient had noticed progressive stiffness of the muscles in both upper and lower limbs. She had also noted difficulty in getting up from a chair and maintaining proper posture. Her movement disorder consisted of a 3 - 6 beats per second tremor at her right wrist and fingers; however, no tremor was found on the left upper limb.

Given the combination of signs and symptoms which of the following best describes this disorder?
neuro_batch3.l: The patient presents with classic Parkinson’s disease features including resting tremor (3–6 Hz), rigidity, bradykinesia, and postural instability.
neuro_batch3.h: Resting tremor (3–6 Hz) + rigidity + posture issues = Parkinson’s

neuro_batch3.q: Which of the following will be noted by the neurologist if tested?
neuro_batch3.l: Cogwheel rigidity is a hallmark of Parkinson’s disease due to increased muscle tone with superimposed tremor.

neuro_batch3.q: Which of the following structures is most likely damaged by the tumor shown in the MRI?
a: Lentiform nucleus, internal capsule, and thalamus
neuro_batch3.l: The tumor location affects both the lentiform nucleus and the adjacent internal capsule due to their close anatomical relationship.
neuro_batch3.h: Basal ganglia + nearby white matter = lentiform + internal capsule

neuro_batch3.q:
A 55 yr old man presents with left eye pain and doodle vision. When at rest his eye deviates downwards and laterally ,as shown in the picture , adduction is impaired in the affected eye. however abduction is intact. Left eye shows dilated pupil and non responsive to the light.

Which of the following cranial nerve is most likely to be injured?
neuro_batch3.l: Oculomotor nerve (CN III) palsy causes a 'down and out' eye, ptosis, impaired adduction, and a dilated non-reactive pupil.
neuro_batch3.h: Down & out + blown pupil = CN III palsy

neuro_batch3.q:
A 66 year old woman developed headaches and vomiting in the past month. She walked on a broad base and often staggered to her left side. The neurologic exam indicated bilateral papilledema. Her pupils reacted normally to light. On attempted lateral gaze to the left, there was nystagmus. Cranial nerve exam was normal. Muscle strength in the limbs, deep tendon responses and muscle tone were reduced slightly on the left. There was also intention tremor, incoordination of finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin tests. There was disorganization in alternating movement tests of the left hand (dysdiadochokinesia). Sensory exam was normal However she demonstrated a positive Romberg sign when standing with heels together with eyes open or closed.

Which of the following regions most likely contains the lesion?
a: Vermal region of the anterior cerebellar lobe
neuro_batch3.l: Cerebellar hemisphere lesions cause ipsilateral limb ataxia, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, and coordination deficits.

neuro_batch3.q: 52 yr old man visits the clinic complaining of difficulty in tasting the food . He also has trouble smiling and not closing the eyes tightly. Through which of the following tracts or Nuclei affected cranial nerve traverse.
neuro_batch3.l: The nucleus tractus solitarius carries taste sensation and is associated with facial nerve (CN VII) pathways.

neuro_batch4.q: The superior cerebellar peduncles carry.
neuro_batch4.l: The superior cerebellar peduncle carries efferent fibers and includes the ventral spinocerebellar tract.

neuro_batch4.q: 63 yr old man comes to physician because had a problem using his arms in useful way as for the past 4 months . On physical examination he found to have postural instability. Cogwheel rigidity in his limbs. and pill rolling rigidity when he is at rest. findings of the patient shown in the Image below . Which of the following pathological finding would you see in this patient ?
neuro_batch4.l: Parkinson’s disease is characterized by Lewy bodies, which are intracellular inclusions composed of alpha-synuclein.

neuro_batch4.q: A 35-year-old man suffered a stroke that did not cause paralysis. However, he discovered that he was unable to perform complex learned movements. The region of the cerebral cortex most likely affected by the stroke was the:
neuro_batch4.l: The premotor cortex is responsible for planning and execution of learned motor activities (apraxia when damaged).

neuro_batch4.q: During what appeared to be routine surgery for a torn ligament, a middle-aged man suffered a stroke. After a few days, the patient showed some recovery because he was able to walk with some difficulty, and, in addition, sensory functions seemed normal. However, a neurologic evaluation revealed a weakness in muscles that regulate breathing, speech, swallowing, and facial expression. A subsequent magnetic resonance imaging scan indicated that the stroke was limited but primarily affected the:
neuro_batch4.l: The genu of the internal capsule contains corticobulbar fibers that control muscles of the face, speech, and swallowing.

neuro_batch4.q: A 67-year-old man was taken to the emergency room after collapsing in a movie theater. The patient regained consciousness and, several days later, displayed a right-side paralysis with pronounced spasticity. The neurologist concluded that the stroke might have involved:
neuro_batch4.l: Lesions of the internal capsule cause contralateral spastic paralysis due to involvement of corticospinal tracts.

neuro_batch4.q: A patient presented with paralysis of the left side of the limbs and left side of the lower face and deviation of the tongue to the left with no atrophy and with no loss of taste sensation. This constellation of deficits most likely resulted from a lesion of the:
a: Ventromedial medulla on the right side
Ventromedial medulla on the left side
neuro_batch4.l: A lesion in the left internal capsule causes contralateral motor deficits including face, limbs, and tongue deviation without LMN signs.

neuro_batch4.q: A 72-year-old right-handed man with a history of atrial fibrillation and diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency department by his family. His son describes that when his father awoke that morning, he was unable to use words or sentences that made any sense. On examination, the patient has spontaneous, fluent speech, with normal grammatical constructs and prosody. However, the majority of what he says is meaningless. He is unable to follow commands, except to close his eyes. When asked to identify objects, he uses inappropriate substitutions of words. He also fails to repeat words and is unable to read. A lesion of which of the following areas would most likely account for his language deficits?
neuro_batch4.l: Wernicke’s aphasia is caused by a lesion in the superior temporal gyrus, leading to fluent but meaningless speech with poor comprehension.

neuro_batch4.q:
A 66 year old woman developed headaches and vomiting in the past month. She walked on a broad base and often staggered to her left side. The neurologic exam indicated bilateral papilledema. Her pupils reacted normally to light. On attempted lateral gaze to the left, there was nystagmus. Cranial nerve exam was normal. Muscle strength in the limbs, deep tendon responses and muscle tone were reduced slightly on the left. There was also intention tremor, incoordination of finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin tests. There was disorganization in alternating movement tests of the left hand (dysdiadochokinesia). Sensory exam was normal However she demonstrated a positive Romberg sign when standing with heels together with eyes open or closed.

Which of the following regions most likely contains the lesion?
a: Vermal region of the anterior cerebellar lobe
neuro_batch4.l: Cerebellar hemisphere lesions cause ipsilateral limb ataxia, intention tremor, and dysdiadochokinesia.

neuro_batch4.q: As a result of a dysfunction in development, a 4-year-old boy has difficulty walking and maintaining balance. It is later determined that there is significant loss of neurons in the cerebellum that disrupts the neuronal organization of the cerebellar glomerulus. Which of the following best characterizes this glomerulus?
a: Climbing fiber terminals, Golgi axons, and granule cell dendrites
Mossy fiber terminals, Purkinje cell axons, and granule cell dendrites
Mossy fiber terminals, purkinje and granule cell dendrites, and Golgi cell axon terminals
Climbing fiber terminals, Golgi cell dendrites, Purkinje cell dendrites, and axon terminals of parallel fibers
Mossy fiber terminals, Golgi axons, and axon terminals of granule cells
neuro_batch4.l: The cerebellar glomerulus consists of mossy fiber terminals, Golgi cell axons, and granule cell dendrites.

neuro_batch4.q: A 64-year-old woman had a cerebrovascular accident 6 months ago. Her past medical history is remarkable for hyperthyroidism and atrial fibrillation. She initially presented with slurred speech and right hemiparesis. The hemiparesis resolved, but her speech is still agrammatic and nonfluent, and she has difficulty finding words and completing sentences. Her comprehension is intact, and she appears frustrated when she attempts to speak. The remainder of the neurologic examination is normal. Which of the following best describes her deficit?
neuro_batch4.l: Nonfluent, agrammatic speech with intact comprehension is characteristic of Broca aphasia.

neuro_batch4.q: Which of the following is considered a primary output of the VA nucleus?
neuro_batch4.l: The ventral anterior (VA) nucleus of the thalamus projects primarily to the prefrontal cortex and is involved in motor planning.

neuro_batch4.q: A 59-year-old male suffered a stroke and later presented with a spastic paralysis. Which of the following structures was affected by the stroke that accounts for the spasticity?
neuro_batch4.l: Spastic paralysis results from upper motor neuron lesions, commonly due to damage to corticospinal fibers in the internal capsule.

neuro_batch4.q: Considering the basal ganglia structures, which one of the following parts are believed to receive all inputs to the basal ganglia?
neuro_batch4.l: The globus pallidus and substantia nigra serve as major output nuclei of the basal ganglia receiving processed input.

neuro_batch4.q: Patients with basal ganglia lesions can have either hyperkinetic or hypokinetic movement disorders. Which of the following diseases cause hyperkinetic uncontrolled involuntary movements which produce a random pattern of jerks and twists?
neuro_batch4.l: Huntington’s disease causes chorea, characterized by random, jerky, hyperkinetic movements.

neuro_batch4.q: After his wife had a stroke, a husband notices that his wife's hair is always tangled and uncombed on the left side of her head, although on the right her hair is straightened and orderly. The left side of her face is usually dirty from food remaining there, but the right face is always clean. She has stopped putting her ring and watch on her left hand. When eating, she never uses her fork on the left side of her plate and, surprisingly, she often asks why she was not given a fork to eat with. This woman's stroke most likely damaged the:
neuro_batch4.l: Contralateral neglect syndrome is caused by damage to the parietal lobe, especially in the nondominant hemisphere.

neuro_batch4.q: Area no 39 is situated in which of the following gyrus?
neuro_batch4.l: Brodmann area 39 corresponds to the angular gyrus, involved in language and spatial cognition.

neuro_batch4.q: The projections of the dorsomedial nucleus of the thalamus are primarily related to:
neuro_batch4.l: The dorsomedial nucleus of the thalamus projects to association cortices including parietal regions involved in higher processing.

neuro_batch4.q: A 45-year-old man is admitted to the emergency department with a worsening headache, nystagmus, and gait ataxia. An MRI of the brain demonstrates a large infarction involving cerebellum. During the neurologic examination, the doctor asks the patient to first touch his nose and then the examiner’s finger. Which of the following phenomena is most likely to be seen with this test?
neuro_batch4.l: Cerebellar lesions cause intention tremor during goal-directed movements such as finger-to-nose testing.

neuro_batch4.q: Considering the basal ganglia structures, which one of the following parts are believed to receive all inputs to the basal ganglia?
neuro_batch4.l: Globus pallidus and substantia nigra are major output structures integrating basal ganglia signals.

neuro_batch4.q: A patient delays initiation of movement, displays an uneven trajectory in moving her hand from above her head to touch her nose, and is uneven in her attempts to demonstrate rapid alternation of pronating and supinating movements of the hand and forearm. Which of the following regions most likely contains the lesion?
a: Hemispheres of the posterior cerebellar lobe
Vermal region of the anterior cerebellar lobe
neuro_batch4.l: Cerebellar hemisphere lesions cause limb ataxia, dysmetria, and dysdiadochokinesia.

neuro_batch4.q: A 39 year-old baseball player noticed the abrupt onset of involuntary movements on his right side. His right arm would make large, violent, flailing or ballistic movements that interfered with his game. The most likely site of the damage was the:
neuro_batch4.l: Hemiballismus is caused by a lesion of the contralateral subthalamic nucleus.

neuro_batch4.q:
Cynthia is a 75-year-old woman who has had high blood pressure and diabetes for more than 15 years. One day, as she was reaching for a switch to put on the light she collapsed. While trying to get up from the floor, she noticed that she was unable to move her right arm or leg. Katherine attempted to cry for help because she was unable to reach the telephone; however, her speech was slurred and rather unintelligible. She lay on the floor and waited for help to arrive. Cynthia’s son began to worry about his usually prompt mother when she didn’t arrive for his Birthday. After several attempts to telephone her apartment without getting an answer, he drove there and found her lying on the floor. She attempted to tell him what had happened, but her speech was too slurred to comprehend. Assuming that his mother had had a stroke, the son called an ambulance to take her to the nearest emergency room (ER). A neurology resident was called to see Cynthia in the emergency room because the physicians there likewise thought that she had a stroke. The resident noted that Cynthia followed commands very well, and, although her speech was very slurred, it was logical in organization. The lower two-thirds of her face drooped on the right. Her tongue pointed to the right side when she was asked to protrude it. Her right arm and leg were severely, but equally, weak; her left side had normal strength. She felt a pin and a vibrating tuning fork equally on both sides.

Where did Cynthia’s stroke most likely happened?
neuro_batch4.l: Pure motor stroke with cranial involvement and no sensory loss suggests internal capsule lesion.

neuro_batch4.q: By looking at the patient's condition and physical findings. What is your diagnosis?
neuro_batch4.l: Resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia indicate Parkinson’s disease.

neuro_batch4.q: Gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA) is considered the predominant neurotransmitter in all of the following except.
neuro_batch4.l: Corticostriatal fibers are glutamatergic, not GABAergic.

neuro_batch4.q: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding the striatum?
a: Receives inhibitory input from the cortex
Consists of the lenticular nucleus and the putamen
Receives Dopaminergic input from the substantia nigra pars compacta
Contains cells that use glutamate as their transmitter
neuro_batch4.l: The striatum receives dopaminergic input from the substantia nigra pars compacta.

neuro_batch4.q: A 45 year old woman was brought to the ED after being found collapsed on the floor by her daughter. When the woman was examined, her deficits included weakness of the left upper and lower limb with increased tone and increased deep tendon reflexes. Her left upper limb was held in a flexed position and her left lower limb was extended. This condition is an example of:
neuro_batch4.l: Upper motor neuron lesions cause spasticity with increased tone and reflexes.

neuro_batch4.q: In the above case, further examination revealed a decreased nasolabial fold and drooping mouth on the left although she blinked both eyes and raised both eye brows normally. Tactile and pin prick sensation were normal and no other symptoms were observed. The most likely location of the lesion would be:
neuro_batch4.l: Facial weakness sparing forehead suggests upper motor neuron lesion likely above facial nucleus, consistent with rostral medulla involvement.

neuro_batch4.q: Which of the following statements is TRUE?
a: Hemiballism occurs ipsilateral to the lesion in the subthalamic nucleus (SUB)
A person with Hemiballism could be helped by administration of a cholinergic agonist
A person with Hemiballism could be helped by administration of a cholinergic antagonist
A person with Hemiballism could be helped by administration of a DA agonist
neuro_batch4.l: Hemiballismus is treated by reducing excessive movement; cholinergic antagonists can help reduce hyperkinesia.

neuro_batch4.q: Which of the following statement is TRUE?
a: Activity in the direct pathway through the basal ganglia results in reduced firing of thalamic neurons (VA/VL)
The striatum is defined as the globus pallidus and the putamen
Cells in the striatum project directly to the VA/VL
neuro_batch4.l: The direct pathway disinhibits the thalamus, increasing movement.

neuro_batch4.q: The following statement is not true in regard to cerebellar lesions.
a: Tremor is present during movements (intention tremor)
The patient is unable to perform rapid alternating movements (adiadochokinesis)
Movements are not fluid but are broken up into separate joint movements (decomposition of movements)
All deficits are Contralateral to the lesion
neuro_batch4.l: Cerebellar lesions produce ipsilateral deficits, not contralateral.

neuro_batch4.q:
Peter is a 62-year-old man, previously healthy, who was brought to a neurologist by his daughter because of increasing difficulty in walking. His daughter noticed that for the past year, he had difficulty getting out of a chair and took a lot of time to begin to walk. When he did walk, he walked with a slow, shuffling gait. In addition, she had noticed some changes in his face, and that he had been drooling excessively. His signature on checks became progressively smaller from the beginning of his name to the end, and he had developed a new tremor. The neurologist examined Peter and noticed immediately that Peter’s facial expression was masklike, with few eye blinks. His speech was soft and monotonous, and he had a slow, resting pill-rolling tremor in both of his hands. He had very little spontaneous movement, and his arms, legs, and trunk were stiff. When the neurologist tried to flex his arm, he felt many catches, similar to a cogwheel. There was no weakness, sensory problems, or abnormalities in his reflexes. When asked to walk, Peter took many tries to rise from his chair. When he finally stood up, his posture was stooped and flexed. His gait was slow, his feet shuffled when he walked, and his arms didn’t swing with his steps.

Damage to which of the following structures causes Peter’s problem with movement?
neuro_batch4.l: Parkinson disease is due to degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra pars compacta.

neuro_batch4.q: In Huntington’s disease, there is a loss of
a: Substance P in the substantia nigra
ACh and GABA in intrastriatal and cortical neurons
neuro_batch4.l: Huntington disease involves degeneration of GABAergic and cholinergic neurons in the striatum.

neuro_batch4.q: On examination, you find that a patient's eyes have the position at rest shown on the right. This is most likely due to a lesion of: (As Shown In the Pic)
neuro_batch4.l: CN III lesion causes 'down and out' eye due to unopposed lateral rectus and superior oblique.

neuro_batch4.q: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding Parkinson’s disease?
neuro_batch4.l: Parkinson disease is characterized by bradykinesia and hypokinesia.

neuro_batch4.q: Since motor dysfunctions associated with disturbances of basal ganglia are expressed on the contralateral side of the body, one may conclude that the basal ganglia project
a: Fibers to the spinal cord that are crossed
Fibers to motor nuclei of the brainstem whose axons then project to the contralateral spinal cord
Fibers to structures that ultimately influence motor regions of the ipsilateral cerebral cortex
Axons to the cerebellum, whose outputs are known to modulate the contralateral side of the body
Fibers directly to the contralateral motor cortex
neuro_batch4.l: Basal ganglia influence ipsilateral cortex, which then controls contralateral body.

neuro_batch5.q: A lesion in the right paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) would most likely result in:
a: Inability of both eyes to look to the right
Inability of the right eye to adduct
Inability of the left eye to abduct
neuro_batch5.l: The PPRF is the horizontal gaze center. A lesion causes ipsilateral gaze palsy, meaning neither eye can look toward the side of the lesion.
neuro_batch5.h: PPRF = horizontal gaze center → both eyes move together

neuro_batch5.q: Which structure must be activated to initiate voluntary gaze to the left?
neuro_batch5.l: Frontal eye fields control contralateral gaze. The right frontal eye field activates the left PPRF to initiate leftward gaze.
neuro_batch5.h: Frontal eye field → opposite direction gaze

neuro_batch5.q: A destructive lesion of the left frontal eye field (Area 8) would cause the eyes to deviate:
neuro_batch5.l: A destructive lesion of the frontal eye field causes the eyes to deviate toward the side of the lesion due to unopposed activity of the opposite side.
neuro_batch5.h: Destructive FEF lesion → eyes look toward lesion

neuro_batch5.q: A lesion of the right medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) would most likely produce which of the following findings when attempting left gaze?
a: Right eye fails to adduct and left eye shows nystagmus
Right eye deviates downward and outward
neuro_batch5.l: In internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO), the eye on the side of the MLF lesion cannot adduct, and the contralateral abducting eye develops nystagmus.
neuro_batch5.h: INO = ipsilateral adduction failure + contralateral nystagmus

neuro_batch5.q: Which feature distinguishes internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) from an oculomotor nerve (CN III) palsy?
neuro_batch5.l: In INO, convergence is preserved because it uses a pathway independent of the MLF, unlike CN III palsy where convergence is impaired.

neuro_batch5.q: A young adult presents with bilateral internuclear ophthalmoplegia. What is the most likely underlying cause?
neuro_batch5.l: Bilateral INO is classically associated with multiple sclerosis, especially in younger patients.

neuro_batch5.q: A lesion affecting both the right PPRF and right MLF would most likely result in which of the following findings?
a: Neither eye can look right, and the right eye cannot adduct on left gaze
Right eye cannot abduct and left eye cannot adduct
neuro_batch5.l: One-and-a-half syndrome combines ipsilateral gaze palsy (PPRF lesion) and INO (MLF lesion). Thus, neither eye looks toward the lesion, and the ipsilateral eye cannot adduct when looking away.

neuro_batch5.q: In one-and-a-half syndrome, which eye movement is preserved?
neuro_batch5.l: The only preserved movement is abduction of the contralateral eye, as the ipsilateral PPRF and MLF are both damaged.
neuro_batch5.h: Only ONE eye moves ONE way

neuro_batch5.q: A patient with one-and-a-half syndrome attempts to look away from the lesion side. What is observed?
a: Ipsilateral eye fails to adduct, contralateral eye abducts with nystagmus
neuro_batch5.l: When looking away from the lesion, the ipsilateral MLF lesion prevents adduction, while the contralateral eye abducts and often shows nystagmus (INO component).

neuro_batch5.q: A lesion in the facial colliculus region of the pons results in one-and-a-half syndrome plus ipsilateral facial paralysis. What is this condition called?
neuro_batch5.l: Eight-and-a-half syndrome is defined as one-and-a-half syndrome plus ipsilateral CN VII palsy due to involvement of the facial nerve fibers looping around the abducens nucleus.

neuro_batch5.q: Which additional deficit distinguishes eight-and-a-half syndrome from one-and-a-half syndrome?
a: Ipsilateral lower motor neuron facial paralysis
neuro_batch5.l: Eight-and-a-half syndrome includes ipsilateral CN VII palsy, resulting in complete facial weakness on the same side.

neuro_batch5.q: A patient presents with inability to gaze toward the right, failure of the right eye to adduct on left gaze, and right-sided facial paralysis involving the forehead. Where is the lesion located?
neuro_batch5.l: This presentation is classic for eight-and-a-half syndrome, which localizes to the ipsilateral pons involving the PPRF, MLF, and CN VII fibers.

neuro_batch5.q: Shining light in one eye normally causes constriction of both pupils. This is due to projections from the pretectal nucleus to:
neuro_batch5.l: The pretectal nucleus sends bilateral projections to both Edinger-Westphal nuclei, producing both direct and consensual pupillary constriction.

neuro_batch5.q: Which structure serves as the afferent limb of the pupillary light reflex?
neuro_batch5.l: The afferent limb of the pupillary light reflex is carried by the optic nerve (CN II), which transmits light information to the pretectal nucleus.

neuro_batch5.q: A lesion of the pretectal nucleus would most likely result in which of the following findings when light is shined in one eye?
a: No direct or consensual pupillary constriction
Normal pupillary constriction in both eyes
neuro_batch5.l: The pretectal nucleus is essential for relaying signals to both Edinger-Westphal nuclei. Damage results in loss of both direct and consensual light reflexes.
neuro_batch5.h: Pretectal lesion → no light reflex at all
